event: Archaic-period Amerindian peoples likely made early visits to or seasonal use of the island during preceramic migrations through the Lesser Antilles.
Arawak-speaking Saladoid peoples migrated from South America and established the first permanent agricultural and pottery-making settlements on the island.
Island-Carib (Kalinago) peoples gradually displaced or absorbed the earlier Arawak inhabitants, naming the island Iouanalao or Hewanorra, 'land of the iguanas.'
Spanish navigators charting the Lesser Antilles recorded the island on early maps, though no Spanish settlement was attempted.
Sixty-seven English colonists bound for Guiana landed after their ship was blown off course, but Kalinago resistance soon reduced the survivors and forced their flight.
An English settlement attempt by colonists from Bermuda and St. Kitts was destroyed by the Kalinago within a few years.
The French West India Company purchased the island, and French settlers from Martinique began establishing a foothold.
The French concluded a treaty with the Kalinago, easing tensions and allowing French colonial expansion on the island.
Thomas Warner led an English expedition from Barbados that briefly occupied the island before disease and resistance forced withdrawal.
The French crown took direct control of the island, annexing it as a dependency of Martinique.
Soufriere was founded as the first organized French town on the island, anchoring the developing plantation society.
Large-scale sugar cultivation worked by enslaved Africans expanded across the island under French planters.
British forces captured the island during the American Revolutionary War, using its harbors as a base against the French fleet.
The Treaty of Paris returned the island to French control following the end of the American Revolutionary War.
Revolutionary France abolished slavery and British forces captured the island, which then descended into the guerrilla conflict known as the Brigands' War.
Free people and formerly enslaved fighters of the 'Brigands' drove British forces from much of the island.
British troops under Sir Ralph Abercromby retook the island and suppressed the Brigand resistance, restoring colonial control and slavery.
Britain captured the island for the final time during the Napoleonic Wars, ending more than a century of contested Franco-British rule.
The Treaty of Paris formally ceded the island to Britain, making Saint Lucia a permanent British colony after changing hands fourteen times.
Slavery was abolished throughout the British Empire, beginning a period of apprenticeship for the island's enslaved population.
Full emancipation ended the apprenticeship system, freeing the island's African-descended population from forced labor.
Saint Lucia was incorporated into the British Windward Islands administration.
Castries became the administrative capital of the British Windward Islands, boosting the town's importance as a coaling station.
A new constitution introduced the first elected members to the colony's legislative council, beginning limited representative government.
Labour unrest across the British West Indies spurred the formation of trade unions and pressure for political reform in Saint Lucia.
A devastating fire destroyed much of central Castries, prompting major rebuilding of the capital.
Universal adult suffrage was introduced, granting all adults the right to vote in colonial elections.
Saint Lucia joined the short-lived West Indies Federation as a constituent territory.
The West Indies Federation collapsed, ending hopes of a unified independent Caribbean state.
The Hess Oil terminal opened, establishing a major petroleum transshipment facility that became an economic mainstay.
Saint Lucia became an associated state of the United Kingdom, gaining full internal self-government with Britain retaining defense and foreign affairs.
Saint Lucia achieved full independence on 22 February as a constitutional monarchy within the Commonwealth, with John Compton as first prime minister.
Saint Lucia was admitted to the United Nations on 18 September as its 152nd member state.
The Saint Lucia Labour Party won the first post-independence election, bringing Allan Louisy to the premiership.
Saint Lucia became a founding member of the Organisation of Eastern Caribbean States.
John Compton's United Workers Party returned to power after a period of political instability, beginning a long era of UWP dominance.
Saint Lucian poet Derek Walcott was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature, bringing international acclaim to the island's culture.
Plummeting banana prices and the threatened loss of preferential European markets triggered a deep crisis in the island's agriculture-dependent economy.
The Saint Lucia Labour Party under Kenny Anthony won a landslide victory, ending fifteen years of UWP rule and accelerating diversification toward tourism.
Tourism firmly overtook bananas as the leading economic sector, reshaping the island's development around hospitality and services.
Founding father Sir John Compton died shortly after returning to office, marking the end of an era in Saint Lucian politics.
Hurricane Tomas struck the island, causing severe flooding, landslides, deaths, and major damage to infrastructure and agriculture.
Allegations of extrajudicial police killings led the United States to suspend assistance to the Royal Saint Lucia Police Force in the 'IMPACS' affair.
Allen Chastanet's United Workers Party won the general election, returning the party to government.
The Saint Lucia Labour Party under Philip J. Pierre won the general election, returning to power amid the COVID-19 pandemic.
Saint Lucia and the wider Commonwealth marked the death of Queen Elizabeth II and the accession of King Charles III as head of state.
